entry.title: Umschalten zwischen Spaces und Groups Launchpad - Parameter SPACES_ENABLE_USER
entry.content:
Wir bauen Schritt für Schritt unser Gruppen-Launchpad auf die neue Bereiche, Seiten und Abschnitte UI Sicht um. dabei wird beabsichtigt dem Nutzer selbst zu ermöglichen auf das neue Menü umzustellen, sofern es für seinen Bereich fertig gestellt wurde. Dazu möchten wir den Standard-Einstieg für den Benutzer auf dem Gruppenmenü belassen und dem Benutzer ermöglichen individuell auf das Bereichsmenü umzuschalten.

Die Parameter SPACES und SPACES_ENABLE_USER sitzen wie folgt:
SPACES = false
SPACES_ENABLE_USER = true

Erwartet würde von mir, das der Benutzer bei der ersten Anmeldung nach dem Setzen dieser Parameter auf seinem Launchpad im bisherigen und von uns gepflegten Gruppenmenü angemeldet wird und die Möglichkeit hat sich auf Wunsch auf Spaces und Pages umzustellen. Erst nach dieser individuellen Umstellung sollte dann für Folgeanmeldungen das Bereichsmenü direkt angezeigt werden.

Das Systemverhalten ist jedoch anders.
Das erste Anmelden ist abhängig von Usereinstellungen, die vor der Freigabe der Bereichsmenüs bereits vorliegen, hatte der Benutzer vor der true-Schaltung bereits mit Bereichen experimentiert, sieht er direkt Bereiche statt d

entry.title: Design an OCM Strategy That Drives SAP Value, Not Just Project Compliance
entry.content:
In the first article, the series established a core truth: SAP transformations succeed or fail based on adoption, not deployment. If that is the "why," then strategy is the "how."

Yet this is where many SAP Organisational Change Management (OCM) efforts begin to unravel. OCM is often reduced to a set of disconnected activities—communications, training, and stakeholder updates—rather than a structured strategy aligned to the SAP programme lifecycle. The result is predictable: strong intent, weak execution, and inconsistent adoption.

This article focuses on how to define an SAP OCM strategy that is practical, measurable, and integrated into the transformation from the start.

SAP's five OCM dimensions as a strategic foundation

SAP's OCM framework is built around five dimensions: change strategy, change leadership, change communication, change realization, and change effectiveness. These dimensions appear explicitly in the SAP User Learning and Change Management Service, which defines, manages, and executes change management deliverables across all five. Practitioners working on S/4HANA or SuccessFactors programmes who commission this service will find it structured around these dimensions rather than around generic communications and training activity alone.

Understanding these five dimensions matters at the strategy stage because it changes what you plan for. A strategy built only around communications and training is incomplete from the outset. A strategy built across all five dimensions has the architecture to handle resistance, leadership drift, measurement gaps, and post-go-live stabilisation—the problems that actually derail transformation adoption.

Why SAP OCM strategies fail in practice

Across S/4HANA programmes, several recurring issues emerge early:

  • OCM is mobilised too late, often during Realize rather than Discover or Prepare.
  • Stakeholder analysis is superficial, missing informal power structures.
  • Governance models focus on design decisions but ignore adoption accountability.
  • Training is positioned as the primary solution to change resistance.
  • Executive alignment is assumed rather than actively maintained.

In one global retail transformation, the OCM strategy consisted of a communication calendar and training plan. What it lacked was clarity on who was accountable for adoption at a regional level. Post-go-live, each region interpreted processes differently, leading to compliance issues and delayed financial close cycles. The lesson is simple: without a clear strategy, OCM becomes activity-driven rather than outcome-driven.

A practical framework: the SAP OCM Strategy Stack

An effective SAP OCM strategy can be structured across five interconnected layers, designed to align with SAP Activate while remaining adaptable to agile delivery models.

Layer 1: Change Vision and Business Outcomes

Define what success looks like beyond system go-live. This includes measurable outcomes such as reduced cycle times, improved data quality, or increased process compliance. In a manufacturing S/4HANA programme, the stated goal was "standardisation." The measurable outcome was a 20 percent reduction in procurement cycle time. This reframing shifted conversations from system features to business impact.

Layer 2: Stakeholder Landscape and Influence Mapping

Move beyond stakeholder lists to understand influence, resistance potential, and political dynamics. Who can accelerate adoption? Who can block it—formally or informally? Where does local autonomy conflict with global design? In practice, middle managers often emerge as the highest-risk group—not because they oppose change, but because they are accountable for operational continuity.

Layer 3: Change Impact and Experience Mapping

Assess how processes, roles, and behaviours will change across the organisation. This includes role-level impact assessments, process deviations from current ways of working, and identification of "moments that matter" in the employee experience. In a Brownfield transformation, even minor UI changes created disproportionate frustration because they affected high-frequency tasks.

Layer 4: Governance and Decision-Making Model

Define how decisions are made, escalated, and enforced—particularly where global standards conflict with local requirements. A common pattern: global design authority defines standardised processes; regions request exceptions based on legitimate constraints; no clear escalation or arbitration mechanism exists. The result is either uncontrolled deviation or delayed decisions, both of which impact adoption.

Layer 5: Adoption Measurement and Feedback Loops

Establish how adoption will be tracked and acted upon. This includes usage metrics (transaction volumes, feature utilisation), user proficiency indicators, sentiment and feedback mechanisms, and business outcome tracking linked to SAP processes. Without this layer, OCM cannot demonstrate value or adjust course during the programme.

 

How the CQC for Implementation Gates reinforces OCM strategy

SAP's Continuous Quality Check (CQC) for Implementation Gates provides a practical mechanism for validating that OCM strategy is being executed rather than merely documented. Available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition across the Prepare, Explore, Realize, and Deploy phases—and for SAP SuccessFactors across Prepare, Explore, Realize, Deploy, and Run—each CQC gate functions as a checklist-based milestone that verifies key deliverables before the programme advances.

For OCM strategy specifically, the Prepare gate is the most critical. This is where the OCM strategy document, stakeholder register, and initial governance model should be in place. A programme that reaches the Prepare gate without a defined adoption accountability structure is likely to struggle in Explore when design decisions begin to generate resistance. Using the CQC gate as a forcing function for OCM strategy completeness—rather than treating it as an administrative step—is a practical way to avoid the late mobilisation problem that appears so frequently in S/4HANA programmes.

 

Connecting strategy to the SAP lifecycle

An effective OCM strategy is not a standalone document. It must align with delivery phases:

Discover and Prepare: Define vision, stakeholders, and governance foundations. The OCM strategy document should be complete before the Prepare CQC gate.

Explore: Validate impacts, challenge assumptions, and surface resistance. The Explore CQC gate checks that impact analysis is in progress and stakeholder engagement is structured.

Realize: Execute enablement, refine messaging, and activate change networks. The Realize CQC gate reviews training readiness and communication coverage.

Deploy: Reinforce leadership alignment and ensure readiness for cutover. The Deploy CQC gate validates that adoption readiness criteria have been met.

Run: Measure adoption, stabilise operations, and sustain change. For SuccessFactors programmes, the Run CQC gate extends this visibility into the post-deployment phase.

 

Case insight: when strategy meets reality

In a global logistics company, the initial OCM strategy emphasised communication and training. Six months into the programme, resistance escalated in key regions. A reassessment revealed that regional leaders were not aligned on the transformation vision, middle managers lacked clarity on how success would be measured, and local process variations were not formally acknowledged. The recovery approach reframed the change vision around operational efficiency metrics, introduced regional adoption dashboards tied to business KPIs, and established a formal governance model for process deviations. Within one release cycle, adoption improved and escalation volumes decreased significantly. The critical insight: strategy must evolve based on feedback, not remain static.

 

What comes next

With a strategy in place, the next challenge is understanding the stakeholder landscape in depth. The next article focuses on stakeholder identification and impact analysis in SAP transformations, including how to map influence, anticipate resistance, and navigate organisational politics. Because in SAP programmes, understanding who drives change is just as important as understanding what is changing.

 

Further resources

SAP Preferred Success customers on S/4HANA Cloud can access the Adoption Journey: Organisational Change Management programme (PS_PROG_S4HANA05_EN) via SAP Learning Hub. The SAP User Learning and Change Management Service is available as part of RISE with SAP for both Greenfield and Brownfield scenarios and provides structured support across all five OCM dimensions from planning through deployment.

 

Additional Blogs

Blog 1 can be found here

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/drive-sap-transformation-success-from-day-one-why-organisational-change/ba-p/14432347

entry.title: Re: Project Kernseife - improving the measurement of Clean Core in ABAP
entry.content:
Hi!
Let me try to answer your question:
1. I'm not sure what you mean, because BADI Implementations are done as a Class implementation, so the code definitly is checked by Kernseife. Also the interface usage is tracked. ENHO is just the "registration" that a BADI is implemented, but we don't see the benefit of tracking that as you can't "use" a standard BADI Implementation ENHO object, just may be inherit from the standard implementation class, etc.
But happy to get your feedback if there are cases where it would make sense. (PR is also welcome :))

2. This whole topic is covered by the "Allowed Enhancement Technologies" Standard Check. So we don't see the point of Kernseife covering that.

3. see Answer to your first question.
4. Again, I'm not sure to which specific scenario you are refering. Currently there are some scenarios, where custom code is not covered by Kernseife, but also not by the "Usage of APIs" check, which we are based on. So unless this is fixed there, we can't fix it (e.g. Custom Code directly inside the Z-Include of a User Exit).
Here the official SAP recommendation is, as far as I know, to not put code directly in the include, but use another class-method or something to put the actual code in there, so it is covered by ATC.

Hope this helps, otherwise we are happy to also discuss Kernseife specific questions/discusssions inside our github repo, so other people can participate.

entry.title: Re: Connection between SAC Analytics Cloud and SAP HANA C...
entry.content:
Hello @Stephany429 ,

No, it's not possible. SAP note 3103542 explicitly confirms: "As of today, it is not possible to create an Import Connection to SAP HANA Cloud."

Your options:

1. Live connection (recommended)
Use the "SAP HANA Cloud" live connection type — cloud-to-cloud, no Cloud Connector or Agent needed. See Live Data Connection to SAP HANA Cloud Using an "SAP HANA Cloud" Connection in the help.

2. OData import (if you need acquired data)
Expose your HANA Cloud data as OData and create an OData Services import connection. See Import Data Connection to OData Services.

Enhancement request:
There is an active enhancement request for native import to HANA Cloud on the SAP Influence tool — currently under "For Long Term Consideration" status. You can vote for it here SAP HANA Cloud - native Import Connection

Hope this helps.

entry.title: Re: Not showing positive quantity in "Material docume...
entry.content:
Dear @Arrikar_Group,

The Material Documents Overview app does display both positive and negative quantities

Below example the Stock Quantity Change column shows stock decreases as negative values and stock increases as positive values, for example:

  • -75.000 M – Stock Decrease
  • 75.000 M, 7.200 M, and 150.000 M – Stock Increase

Therefore this is not a general limitation of the app. If positive quantities are missing in a specific case, please check the active filters and saved variants, especially Stock Change Type as these may restrict the displayed results

 

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Ability to Group values by Accounting Doc while retaining navigation to Journal Entry Details
entry.content:
When using the Display G/L Account Line Items application, the system displays all individual line items posted to a given G/L account within each accounting document.

The customer would like to analyze the information at accounting document level, showing the aggregated amount per accounting document, instead of displaying every individual line item separately.

When grouping by Accounting Document, the system correctly calculates and displays the aggregated amount per accounting document.

However, the grouped records are displayed in collapsed groups and the user loses the ability to directly navigate from the grouped view to the journal entry/accounting document details.

As a result, users must expand the groups or switch views, which makes the analysis less efficient.

The customer would like to display the total amount per accounting document while still maintaining direct navigation to the underlying accounting document (journal entry).

  1. Is there any standard functionality (for example, Totals, Subtotals, or another analytical feature independent from Group By) that allows users to display aggregated amounts per accounting document without collapsing the line items and without losing drill-down/navigation capability?

  2. When using Group By → Accounting Document, is there a standard way to keep the navigation link to the journal entry details available, either at group level or after expanding the groups?

  3. Is there an alternative standard SAP S/4HANA Cloud application (for example, Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items in General Ledger, or another reporting application) that provides:

    • Aggregation by accounting document,
    • Display of total amount per document,
    • Direct navigation to the journal entry details?

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Ability to Group values by Accounting Doc while retai...
entry.content:
Hi,

The feature you described can be satisfy using app Journal Entry Analyzer. With this app, it is possible to set Company Code, G/L Account, and Journal Entry as Row, with fields you want to aggregate as columns, such as Amount in Company Code Currency. 

That way, the amount is sum by the value of column fields, while it is still allow navigation to a Journal Entry by right click and select Navigate To > Display Journal Entry - New Version, or any other app available for that object.

Best Regards,

An

SAP Support.

entry.title: Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA) - Always Up-to-Date Intraday
entry.content:
As part of our continuous investment in SAP Commodity Management, Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA) has been introduced to significantly improve scalability, performance, and transparency in risk management.

In highly volatile markets, visibility into current positions is critical to:

  • React quickly to market changes
  • Validate positions before End-of-Day (EOD)
  • Ensure compliance with risk limits

 

How Do We Achieve Market Leading Transparency to Intraday Position?

ACRA follows a delta-based intraday calculation approach, leveraging EOD results as a baseline.

Both EOD and Intraday results are available within unified analytical views, enabling users to:

  • View current intraday positions
  • Compare intraday positions vs. EOD
  • Analyse net deltas (changes during the day)

Figure 1: Current Position (C) vs. End-of-Day Position (E)

Figure 2: Net Delta = Intraday (C) – EOD (E)  [displayed by removing the Snapshot Type as Attribute]

 

To achieve such Intraday views in seconds, we take the following approach:

  1. EOD Snapshot Initialization

    • End-of-Day risk calculations provide the baseline dataset.
    • An intraday key figure table is initialized by copying EOD results per snapshot group.

  2. Delta-Based Updates Instead of Full Recalculation

    • Only changed transactions are processed:
      • New transactions (e.g., new contracts)
      • Updated transactions (e.g., quantity/price changes)
      • Removed transactions (e.g., cancellations)

  3.  No Reprocessing of Unchanged Data

    • Transactions without changes remain untouched, avoiding unnecessary recalculations.

 Example

  • EOD Position (Oct 15): Transactions 42001, 42002, 42003
  • Intraday (Oct 16, 10:05):

Transaction

Status

Action

42001

Unchanged

Reused without recalculation

42002

Updated

Quantity adjusted

42003

Deleted

Removed from position

42004

New

Added to position

➡️Result: Efficient, near real-time updates with minimal processing effort.

 

Core Features

1. Intraday Risk Calculation

  • Initialized from EOD snapshots (by snapshot group)
  • Continuous delta updates from:
    • Logistics flows (contracts, nominations, movements)
    • Derivatives (new or amended)
  • Lightweight computation model enabling frequent refresh cycles (e.g., every few minutes)

 

2. Intraday Risk Error Monitoring

  • Centralized collection of business errors
  • Structured reporting via SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Enables fast issue identification and resolution

3. Intraday Risk Calculation Monitoring

  • Real-time visibility into processing status
  • Monitoring by snapshot group
  • Early detection of delays or failures

4. Intraday Risk Reporting

  • Immediate visibility into all position-relevant changes:
    • Contracts
    • Nominations
    • Movements
    • Derivatives
  • Full consistency with last EOD snapshot
  • Ensuring traceability and auditability

 

Key Benefits

Real-Time Position Visibility

  • Continuous updates throughout the day
  • Reduces risk of limit breaches and unintended exposure

Faster Decision-Making

  • Immediate insight enables:
    • Timely hedging
    • Inventory balancing
    • Strategy adjustments

Improved Business Agility

  • React instantly to:
    • Market volatility
    • Logistics disruptions
    • Counterparty changes

High Performance and Scalability

  • Delta-based processing avoids full recalculations
  • Supports high-frequency updates without performance degradation

Governance & Transparency

  • Superior scalability enabling analyses to position across entire organization
  • Integrated monitoring (errors + processing status)
  • Snapshot-level traceability
  • Fully auditable intraday reporting process

 

Summary

ACRA transforms commodity risk management by combining:

  • EOD stability with
  • Intraday responsiveness

Through a delta-based architecture, it delivers:

👉High-frequency updates
👉Scalable performance
👉Full transparency

— enabling organizations to stay in control even in fast-moving markets.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @TO: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @TO: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Waiting                  // transitions to next player in Week 3
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @TO: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Finished
    action winGame();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

 

I decided to keep the same TurnStatus to try with default lifecycle steps for now.

entry.title: Re: SAP ABAP training advice please.
entry.content:
Hi Andy,

I've developed SAP ABAP for too many years and noticed the same.

The one thing that helped me navigate the training is that the majority of traditional ABAP Training now sits under "Back-end Developer" as @marcobuescher has pointed out a good start is acquiring core abap skills which sits under the and results ultimately in certification of back-end developer. The front-end stuff now that we are in cloud could be Fiori, UI5 or any other UI but the core of abap programming now seems to sit under back-end. This is essentially the same as a Developer would have done on SAP GUI back in the day but now is done using Eclipse, taking into account the newer commands. Hope this helps. Regards. Jodh

entry.title: Upcoming Webinar: SAP Business Network Supplier Managed Gateway and Error Handling
entry.content:
The SAP Ariba Supply Chain Collaboration team is delighted to announce an upcoming customer webinar that will provide comprehensive insights into the Supplier Managed Gateway and Error Handling in SAP Business Network. This session will cover the flow of documents from external supplier systems to the Supplier Managed Gateway, introduction to the Managed Gateway, the Document Validator tool, retry processes, Transaction Tracker functionality, document status management, and how to resend documents. Additionally, we will discuss common Supplier Managed Gateway errors and their resolutions, including certificate-related issues.

This session will be held on August 12, 2026. It is expected to last approximately 45 minutes and will also be recorded.

To secure your spot for this informative webinar, please visit the below registration link, according to your region. Once registered, and you are unable to attend, you will receive the recording of the webinar to enjoy in your own time. 

Choose the time that works best for you (same content in both sessions):

Click here to register for Option 2 on August 12th 10:30am EDT / 11:30am BRT / 3:30pm BST

Who should attend?

  • Supplier users
  • Supplier administrators
  • Partners
  • Supply chain professionals

We look forward to your participation in this webinar!

Visit our Features in Focus Webinar calendar and Hub to stay up to date with all things related to our webinars and SBN!

entry.title: Q2 2026: Quarterly Release Highlights for SAP BTP Security and Identity & Access Management
entry.content:
The world of security never stands still, and neither do we. Here's a quick look at the latest SAP BTP Security and Identity & Access Management updates from this quarter, with the features and improvements worth adding to your radar.  

For a complete overview of feature deliveries for SAP Cloud Identity Services, check out our list of all new feature announcements in the SAP Cloud Identity Services Release Notes on the SAP Help Portal.

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Technical users

SAP Cloud Identity Services now supports technical users—a new user type designed for non-human identities that enable secure, automated system-to-system communication and integrations. Technical users are managed separately from regular users through the new Technical Users tile under Users & Authorizations in the administration console, making it easier to create, organize, and maintain service identities. For step-by-step instructions,  see Managing Technical Users.

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Block and delete users

Keeping your Identity Directory clean has just become easier. You can configure SAP Cloud Identity Services to automatically delete users who were created but never signed in to an application, helping reduce inactive accounts and simplify user lifecycle management. We’ve extended this functionality by adding a grace period. For configuration details, check Block or Delete Users Due to Inactivity.

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Automatic creation of Identity Provisioning tenants on SAP Cloud Identity for Neo tenants

Starting June 10, 2026, SAP automatically creates a new Identity Provisioning tenant on the SAP Cloud Identity infrastructure for every existing Identity Provisioning tenant running on the Neo environment that is linked to a shared IAM tenant with Identity Authentication.

This automatic provisioning gives you access to the latest Identity Provisioning capabilities and configuration options available on the SAP Cloud Identity infrastructure—features that are not supported on Neo. The newly created tenant is used for integrations related to bundled SAP cloud solutions, helping prepare your landscape for future enhancements. For migration details, see Migrate Identity Provisioning Bundle Tenant.

SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI: Now available in China

Organizations in China can now subscribe to the SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI and run the service on infrastructure located within China. This availability helps customers meet local deployment requirements while modernizing authentication for SAP GUI. They will be able to simplify integration by connecting more easily with enterprise identity providers, and strengthen security with enhanced support for multifactor authentication (MFA).

You can see the supported data centers here. For more information on the SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI, check the SAP Help Portal.

Application Vulnerability Report for SAP BTP

In December 2025, SAP introduced the Application Vulnerability Report (beta) for SAP BTP, providing an API-driven way to identify open-source vulnerabilities in your Cloud Foundry applications.

The service has now been enhanced with its first graphical user interface, available directly in the SAP BTP Cockpit. The new interface features an intuitive findings dashboard with a severity breakdown, along with a detailed findings view that provides actionable remediation guidance to help you address identified vulnerabilities.

The Application Vulnerability Report is currently available as a beta service in the EU10 region. While subscriptions are not yet available in other SAP BTP landscapes, the service's scanning scope has been expanded significantly. It now scans applications deployed across 42 Cloud Foundry landscapes worldwide, including regions in the United States, Asia Pacific, Japan, Brazil, and additional European landscapes beyond the original EU10 region.

Stay in the loop

Join the SAP BTP Security and SAP Cloud Identity Services communities to get updates, share feedback, and connect with others!

entry.title: While Outbound delivery creation, system checking available stock
entry.content:
Hi Experts,
Good day.

We have developed a background job (with the help of our technical team) to automatically create outbound deliveries for the schedule lines of Sales Scheduling Agreements in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

During delivery creation, we observed that the system creates outbound deliveries only for the quantity that is currently available in stock.

For example:

  • Available stock: 5

  • Schedule line quantity: 10

In this case, the background job creates an outbound delivery for 5 units, while the remaining 5 units remain open.

We observed the same behavior when creating outbound deliveries manually using VL10E. Therefore, I initially assumed that this is the standard SAP behavior, where the system checks stock availability during outbound delivery creation and creates deliveries only for the available quantity.

However, one of my senior colleagues mentioned that he has seen scenarios where the system created an outbound delivery for the entire schedule line quantity, even though sufficient stock was not available.

For example:

  • Available stock: 5

  • Schedule line quantity: 10

In that scenario, the outbound delivery was created for the full 10 units, despite only 5 units being available in stock.

Unfortunately, he is unsure under which circumstances or in which system this behavior was observed.

I would like to understand the following:

  1. Is it standard SAP behavior in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to always consider available stock during outbound delivery creation?

  2. Can this behavior be influenced by configuration or master data in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?

  3. If yes, which configurations or settings control this behavior? For example:

    • Any SSCUI or configuration available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

    • Any other relevant settings

  4. Are there any SAP Notes, SAP Help documentation, blogs, or SAP Community posts that explain this behavior in detail?

I would appreciate any guidance or references that can help me understand this process more clearly.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: SQLAnywhere - SQL Remote Agent not applying messages
entry.content:
Have you tried to use dbremote with the -v option to display verbose output? It should show the contents of the messages. IIRC, the first digit within the message number ("1-...") might indicate that the messages by "STORE17" are being resent, and the receiver might ignore them by design as they have already by applied.
What does SYSREMOTEUSER show for both remotes? Are there differences according to receive_count vs. rereceive_count? Does the table show that the according log offsets have already been confirmed by the current database?

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud | Workspace and Tenant
entry.content:
Dear @quocdt5,

Question 1.

A Workspace is a configuration container in SAP Central Business Configuration. It is used to define and deploy the business scope, organizational structure, finance settings and product-specific configuration to an assigned SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant. It is not a separate SAP system or tenant

More details below:

SAP Learning Setting Up the Implementation Workspaces

Question 2.

Is the general SAP Central Business Configuration term. In a 3-system landscape, two implementation workspaces are normally created:

  • one connected to the Customizing Tenant and
  • one connected to the Development Tenant

The term Development Workspace therefore generally refers to the workspace whose deployment target is the Development Tenant. The required scope and configuration must be activated separately in each workspace.

More details below:

SAP Learning Setting Up the Implementation Workspaces

Question 3.

The Development Tenant provides access to the SAP S/4HANA Cloud ABAP Environment and is primarily used for developer extensibility.

Typical activities include:

  • ABAP Cloud development in ADT for Eclipse
  • Custom CDS views and business objects
  • Custom services and SAP Fiori applications
  • Released BAdI implementations
  • Software component, package, and developer transport management

It is mainly intended for developers rather than day-to-day functional configuration.

More details below:

SAP Learining Requesting SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Systems

SAP Help Tenants in the Development System

Question 4.

The Customizing Tenant is primarily used for:

  • Business configuration and SSCUIs
  • Key-user extensibility
  • Custom fields and custom logic
  • Communication arrangements
  • Business roles and authorizations
  • Data migration and functional testing
  • Customizing and software collection transports

The main difference is that the Customizing Tenant is used for functional configuration and key-user extensibility, while the Development Tenant is used for ABAP Cloud developer extensibility. Both are technically separate clients within the same development system

More details below:

SAP Help Tenants in the Development System

Question 5.

In a standard 3-system landscape, the relationship is:

The workspaces manage and deploy configuration to their assigned tenants. Configuration and extensions are then transported from the development system to the test system and subsequently to production

More details below:

SAP Help Set Up SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in a 3-System Landscape

SAP Learning Setting Up the Implementation Workspaces

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: FAQ: Utilities Sales Process in SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud V2
entry.content:
Your webinar questions answered: SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 Utilities Sales Process, UPIL integration, SAP S/4HANA backend, roadmap, and more.

On July 7, we hosted an SAP Espresso Session to introduce the new CX Utilities Sales Process. The session covered how utilities can modernize and streamline sales processes by enabling sales workflows directly from the SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 agent desktop, creating tailored utility sales orders with relevant product recommendations, and seamlessly integrating with SAP S/4HANA Utilities processes.

The guided energy sales approach connects previously fragmented steps into a single end-to-end workflow, helping utilities improve efficiency, accelerate response times, and enhance overall sales performance. The session also covered the SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 Utilities Add-On, key utility sales use cases, and included a live demonstration of the streamlined process in action.

You can access the session recording here: Utilities CX Reloaded: The New Sales Process Explained

During the session, several questions were raised. To ensure that everyone can benefit from the discussion, we have compiled the questions and answers below.

Our answers are based on our understanding of the questions. Any forward-looking statements should be considered as ROADMAP. For more, please refer to SAP Legal Notice for SAP Materials.

 

FAQ

1. Product Catalog and UPIL

Several attendees asked about how products are defined and managed within the Utilities Sales Process. The following questions cover the role of UPIL as the product catalog, its relationship to the SAP S/4HANA Utilities rate model, and how product maintenance works in practice.

Q: Are the products and plans coming from UPIL or SD materials?

A: Products in the Utilities Sales Process are sourced from UPIL, in SAP S/4HANA. UPIL acts as the product catalog for commodity offerings (electricity, gas, water) and exposes those products to SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 through the Utilities Add-On integration layer. Products are regular materials in SAP S/4HANA.

Q: Which component in SAP S/4HANA Utilities integrates with this sales order: Rate Category or UPIL?

A: The integration point is UPIL, not Rate Category directly. UPIL abstracts the underlying IS/U rate structures and presents them as sellable products in the catalog. That said, UPIL products are ultimately backed by the ISU rate and contract account configuration in SAP S/4HANA, so the link to Rate Categories exists — it is simply mediated through UPIL rather than exposed directly in the SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 UI.

Q: For UPIL products: does SAP provide a UI for defining them, or is only an API available?

A: The UI for defining UPIL products are the regular material transactions in SAP S/4HANA. It is also possible to create materials via APIs.

2. Non-Commodity Products and Complex Sales

Non-commodity products and complex sales scenarios were among the most frequently asked topics during the webinar. The questions below explain the distinction between the Simple Sales and Complex Sales tracks in the Utilities Add-On and clarify what each track is designed to handle.

Q: Can you create a configurable product in a sales order?

A: Configurable products are handled through the Complex Sales process, which follows a different route from the guided selling process covered in this webinar. The Complex Sales process leverages SAP CPQ to handle product configuration, bundling, and pricing for more sophisticated offerings. The guided Utilities Sales Process in the Add-On is specifically designed for the Simple Sales scenario — selecting from predefined UPIL-based commodity products and one premise with a streamlined, step-based flow. If your use case involves configurable products, commodity, non-commodity, multi-premise, CPQ-based pricing, or complex bundles, it would fall under the Complex Sales track.

Q: Can we expect complex sales, such as bundling solar panels or batteries, to be covered by this guided selling process?

A: Not by the guided Utilities Sales Process covered in this webinar, but complex sales scenarios are addressed through a separate Complex Sales process that already exists. The guided process is specifically designed for the Simple Sales scenario — commodity products such as electricity and gas supply contracts. Bundled propositions that combine a commodity with a non-commodity product (solar PV, home battery, EV charging) fall under the Complex Sales track, which leverages SAP CPQ for configuration, pricing, and quoting.

Q: Can we use this process to model non-commodity products like Services, and trigger follow-up processes like credit memos or incentives from SAP S/4HANA?

A: The guided Utilities Sales Process is designed for commodity-based sales and does not cover non-commodity services in its current form. Scenarios involving service products, credit memos, incentive management, or other non-commodity downstream processes in SAP S/4HANA belong to the Complex Sales track, which will follow a CPQ-based approach.

Q: Does this also support non-commodity products alongside commodities?

A: Not within the guided Simple Sales process. Non-commodity products are part of the Complex Sales track, which uses SAP CPQ and is covered separately from the Add-On's guided selling process.

Q: Are there two separate inputs for simulated consumption: one for gas and one for electricity?

A: Yes. The consumption simulation step handles gas and electricity independently. In a dual-fuel scenario, the process supports separate consumption inputs for each commodity, and the simulation logic feeds into the product recommendation and pricing presentation for each individually.

3. SAP S/4HANA Backend Integration and Replication

Several questions focused on how the Utilities Sales Contract integrates with the SAP S/4HANA backend and how data is replicated between the two systems. The answers below clarify the integration architecture and what happens to contract data once the guided sales process is completed.

Q: Does the Utilities Sales Contract live only in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, or does it get replicated to SAP S/4HANA?

A: The Utilities Sales Contract is actually created and managed in SAP S/4HANA. Once the agent completes the guided sales process in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, this triggers the relevant SAP S/4HANA flows, which create the utility sales contract in the backend. The utility sales contract is visualized in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 through online calls. SAP S/4HANA is therefore the system of record for the utility sales contract, with SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 serving as the system of engagement where the guided process takes place.

Q: Is the SAP S/4HANA integration using the regular Sales Contract integration in the SD order?

A: The integration does not follow the standard SD Sales Contract route. As shown in the Order-to-Cash process diagram, the Utilities Sales Order created as part of the guided sales process is passed via API into SAP S/4HANA, where it flows through the Customer Engagement Process Framework into a Utilities Sales Contract. From there, it continues through the UPIL runtime into the IS-U Contract, Billing, and Invoicing layers. This is a Utilities-native integration path, purpose-built around UPIL and the IS-U contract model, rather than the generic SD Sales Contract mechanism.

 

Q: Are the sales objects in the demo Utilities Add-On objects only, or backed by an SD sales order?

A: All the sales objects reside in SAP S/4HANA and are visualized in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 via online calls.

Q: When changing the contract start date, will the change be replicated to utilities artifacts like the contract and installation in SAP S/4HANA?

A: Since we are not storing the utility sales contract in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, all changes are made as online calls to SAP S/4HANA.

4. Market and Regulatory Scope

Some attendees raised questions about which market types the Utilities Sales Process is suited for and how it fits within different system landscapes. The questions below address the applicability to deregulated and regulated markets, as well as the recommended system architecture.

Q: Is this sales process applicable to deregulated markets only, or regulated markets as well?

A: The Utilities Sales Process is primarily designed for deregulated energy markets, where the utility or energy retailer actively sells commodity products in a competitive context. Nonetheless, nothing technically prevents use in a regulated market — the utility sales contract would still be used, just without integration to any market communication.

5. Process Configuration and Customization

In this part, we will cover the topic of extensibility and configurability of the guided sales process. The questions below cover custom validations and the Sales Process Monitor.

Q: Can we add SAP S/4HANA validations after each step in the sales process, like we can in the SAP Service Cloud Version 2 Move process?

A: The step-based architecture of the Utilities Sales Process is built on the same guided process framework as the Move process in SAP Service Cloud Version 2. The extensibility model, including the ability to inject validations or additional logic between steps, follows the same pattern.

Q: Is the Sales Process Monitor the same function as the Process Monitor in Customer Engagement?

A: Yes, the Sales Process Monitor uses the same data as the Process Monitor in Customer Engagement. Supervisors and back-office users using Customer Engagement would be accessing the same data as the agent using the Sales Process Monitor in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 and SAP Service Cloud Version 2.

6. Terms and Conditions

Q: How are Terms and Conditions communicated to the customer?

A: The Terms and Conditions step presents the relevant contractual terms to the customer during the sales interaction in a channel-appropriate format — for example, on-screen during an agent-assisted call, or as a digital document in a self-service or e-commerce context. In the demo we showed, the idea is for the terms and conditions to be read back to the customer on the phone.

Q: What exactly happens in the Terms and Conditions step, and what can be configured there?

A: The Terms and Conditions step is a dedicated stage in the guided flow where the customer acknowledges acceptance of product-specific and general contractual terms before the contract is finalized. Configurable elements include which T&C documents or text blocks are presented and the acceptance mechanism (checkbox, signature, verbal confirmation logging). This step feeds into the review and signature steps that follow, forming the legal closure of the sales interaction.

7. Licensing and Access

Q: Do I need the SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 license, or is SAP Service Cloud Version 2 sufficient?

A: Yes, you will need SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 along with the Utilities Add-On.

Q: When will these features be available, and do customers and partners have access to the beta?

A: As announced at SAP Sapphire, the Utilities Sales Process is planned to be available in August 2026.

8. Case Management and Customer Data

Q: Linking the Utilities Sales Contract to a Case: is this the Case Management in SAP Service Cloud Version 2, not BPEM Case in SAP S/4HANA?

A: Correct. When a Utilities Sales Contract is linked to a Case in the SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 context, that refers to the Case Management capability within SAP Service Cloud Version 2 (the CX-side case object), not Business Process Exception Management (BPEM) cases in SAP S/4HANA.

Q: What role does the SAP Customer Data Platform (CDP) play in these processes?

A: SAP Customer Data Platform is not a direct component of the Utilities Sales Process in its initial form, but it is a relevant part of the broader SAP CX architecture for utilities. SAP Customer Data Platform can enrich the customer profile available to the agent during the sales interaction, surfacing propensity scores, next-best-offer signals, or consolidated interaction history from multiple channels. If your organization has SAP Customer Data Platform deployed, integrating it with SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 can improve the quality of the guided selling interaction.

9. Roadmap and Sessions

Q: Are you planning a Complex Sales Espresso session?

A: If there is interest in this session, please let us know (leave a comment in the blog) — we will gladly organize one.

What's Next

The Utilities Sales Process represents a significant step forward in SAP's utilities-specific CX story, bringing a structured, guided selling experience into SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 that is natively connected to the SAP S/4HANA Utilities backend through UPIL and Sales Contract replication.

If you are a customer or partner interested in seeing the solution in depth, please reach out to your respective Account Executive and we can schedule a follow-up.

Thank you again to everyone who attended the webinar and submitted such thoughtful questions. Your feedback directly shapes the direction of this solution.

Steen and Tijana

entry.title: Re: Partnerbanktyp aus F-47 nicht im F110 Echtlauf

entry.title: Re: How to detect inactive DTPs in SAP BW Process Chains
entry.content:
SAP BW and SAP BW/4HANA already has a failsafe for inactive DTP-s in process chains. There is an automatic activation of inactive DTP-s when the DTP is triggered by a chain:

See CL_RSBK_DTP_API->EXECUTE in BW/4HANA and CL_RSBK_DTP->IF_RSPC_EXECUTE~EXECUTE in SAP BW:

* ---- activate DTP and (if necessary) transformation -----
        l_r_dtp->enqueue_dtp_for_request( ).
        l_r_dtp->set_activated_from_pc( rs_c_true ).
        l_r_dtp->activate_before_execute( ).

 

Of course if for whatever reason the DTP cannot be activated or if the DTP is not existing in A and M versions, then also the chain will not run.

entry.title: Why we need to write more about our SAP Teaching Experiences
entry.content:
1. We Are Creative—but Are We Documenting Enough?

One challenge for teachers and researchers is simple: we are very busy.

We do many creative things in our classrooms, projects, and universities, but sometimes it is difficult to stop, look back, and systematize all the small pieces of information that make up an experience. Time is limited, and daily demands quickly take over.

I have always appreciated the model used at LAC 2024 and LAC 2025. We had the incentive to organize our experiences into short texts—and, for those of us who often need to write scientific articles, a short paper can feel like a blessing =)—reflect on them, and present them to the SAP University Alliances community.

Preparing a presentation forced us—in a very positive way—to transform individual activities, ideas, results, and lessons learned into a coherent story.

 

2. Deadlines Can Be a Good Motivation 😃

And, let's be honest: teachers and researchers seem, by nature, to be moved by deadlines. 😃

At LAC 2026, the model changed, and we did not have the same opportunity to present our teaching experiences. However, another opportunity appeared: the SAP Excellence Membership – Elevating Academic Partnership Through Recognition.

The SAP Excellence Membership recognizes outstanding lighthouse institutions in the SAP University Alliances network in the areas of Educational Excellence, Research Excellence, and Co-Innovation Excellence.

When the call opened, we saw it as an opportunity to look back.

Preparing our application required collecting many different pieces of information that was lost in cyberspace and computers: curriculum initiatives, teaching activities, events, innovative uses of SAP technologies, student learning experiences, and our engagement with SAP University Alliances offerings.

At first, these seemed like separate activities.

But when we started organizing and systematizing them, the pieces began to connect.

We could see a story.

 

3. Looking Back Helped Us See a Story (or sensemaking the journey)

Happily, this process of looking back and systematizing our experiences for the assessment contributed to UCS receiving the 2026 SAP University Alliances Educational Excellence Membership, recognizing our work with curriculum, academic activities, and innovative approaches to incorporating SAP technologies into teaching.

I am very grateful to Maria Calzada and the entire SAP University Alliances team for their support and for creating opportunities that encourage universities to reflect on and share their academic work.

To my surprise, our LinkedIn post about this recognition was also connected to the SAP Community through the post Recognizing Educational Excellence: UCS Honored at... - SAP Community

This was another small example of what can happen when we document and share our experiences.

One piece of content can connect to another. One experience can become part of a broader story.

 

4. A Coffee Conversation and a Simple Question

Another important reflection on the power of writing and systematizing came from a simple coffee conversation at LAC 2026. During the event, I had the opportunity to sit down for a coffee and chat with Katharina Schäfer, and one of the topics we discussed was exactly this: sharing our SAP experiences.

I talked about the LAC 2024 and 2025 model and how valuable it had been to have an incentive to organize and present our work. At one point, she asked me:

“Why don't you write more in our community blog posts?”

The question stayed with me.

I did not know the new community platform very well, but after exploring it, I found it quite interesting and easy to use.

More importantly, Katharina's question made me think about how many valuable experiences remain only in our classrooms, universities, presentations, computers, or personal files.

And perhaps we need to learn to write in a more agile way.

But first, we may need to change our mindset.

Perhaps we think an experience is too small to become an article.

Perhaps the project is not yet complete and we are afraid to talk about incomplete things.

Perhaps we are waiting until we have a “perfect” story to tell.

But maybe we should think differently—and more creatively.

After all, today we have many tools that can help us get started. AI can support the writing process. Voice-to-text can help us capture an idea after a class or an event. Notes, recordings, and other digital tools can help us transform scattered thoughts into a first draft. The technology is available. Maybe the bigger challenge is simply creating the habit of documenting our experiences.

 

5. Write Something. Share Something.

So, my invitation to the SAP University Alliances community, in this first article, is simple:

Write something. Share something.

I saw so many interesting cases at LAC 2026, and I am sure that in every university connected to SAP there is at least one valuable experience to share. That´s a lot of experiences given the number of universities and teachers evolved. These small pieces of experience, when connected through a community, can inspire new projects, encourage collaboration, and even promote new pedagogical models.

As I said before: professors are very creative.

So, let´s write about one teaching experience.

A classroom activity. A student project. An event. An experiment with SAP technology. Something that worked—or even something that did not work as expected. It does not need to be a large research paper or a long article.

Start small. Write something. Share something.

Over time, these small records can become a valuable collection of ideas, reflections, and lessons learned. They may inspire new projects, collaborations, research questions, or more structured academic publications.

Most importantly, they may help another teacher and the students. And most fundamentally:

Sharing and learning are essential to strengthening a digital community.

We already create many interesting experiences in the SAP University Alliances community. Perhaps we just need to write a little more about them.

So, what SAP teaching experience could you share today?

 

Disclaimer

GenAI was used to assist writing, but all the script, refinements, ideas and goodwill remain human 😃
